metric_value: Digital's strategy is to be a leader in transaction processing
notes: Foreword by Carlos G. Borgialli, Senior Manager DECtp Software Engineering; asserts TP is one of the largest most rapidly growing segments of the computer industry

metric_value: Six components: application program, resource manager, transaction manager, communication manager, presentation manager, request manager
notes: DECdta architecture defines modularization and distribution structure common to DECtp products; based on client/server computing model

metric_value: Atomicity, Serializability, Durability — the three transaction properties
notes: Foundational definitions presented at outset of DECdta paper; commit and abort operations defined

metric_value: Embedding transaction semantics in VMS kernel provides consistency, interoperability, and flexibility across all applications not limited to traditional TP
notes: Three stated design goals: pervasive availability/reliability, operating environment consistency, flexibility and interoperability

metric_value: DECdtm uses optimized variant of 2PC; VAXcluster capabilities greatly reduce potential for blocking versus traditional 2PC
notes: Blocking problem between phases 1 and 2 identified as inherent weakness of all 2PC protocols; VAXcluster optimization is key DEC differentiator
ACMS first introduced by Digital in 1984; addresses large complex TP applications; uses 4GL task definition language
Originally developed by third-party vendor; purchased and introduced by Digital in 1988; installed in major financial institutions and manufacturing sites

metric_value: ACMS uses 4GL task definition language (divide and conquer); DECintact uses 3GL library calls; persistent difference will be different application programming interfaces
notes: Both monitors layered on VMS; functional overlap is large; queuing models differ: ACMS deferred task model vs DECintact message-based model

metric_value: DECforms is first implementation of ANSI/ISO Forms Interface Management Systems standard (CODASYL FIMS)
notes: Implements DECdta presentation manager component; provides device-independent interface

metric_value: DECdta architecture supports X/Open TP Working Group standard APIs and IEEE POSIX; OSI-TP wire protocols for multi-vendor interoperability
notes: Standards alignment is explicitly stated as key to DECdta architecture; interfaces will be published and aligned with industry standards as appropriate

metric_value: Future DECdtm services designed to conform to de facto and international standards for transaction processing; ensures VMS application interoperability with other vendors
notes: Laing Johnson Landau paper explicitly states DECdtm interfaces to be made available to any developer or customer

metric_name: TPC Benchmark A — VAX 9000 Model 210 tpsA-Local
notes: Audited TPC Benchmark A result; back-end: 1x VAX 9000 Model 210, 256 MB RAM, 16x RA92 disks, VMS 5.4, VAX ACMS V3.1, VAX Rdb/VMS V4.0, DECforms V1.2; 10x VAXserver 3100 front-end; 90th-percentile response time 1.74 seconds; average 1.20 seconds

metric_name: TPC Benchmark A — VAX 4000 Model 300 tpsA-Local
notes: Audited TPC Benchmark A result; back-end: 1x VAX 4000 Model 300, 64 MB RAM, 18x RF31 disks, VMS 5.4, VAX ACMS V3.1, VAX Rdb/VMS V4.0, DECforms V1.2; 3x VAXserver 3100 front-end; average response time 1.39 seconds; 90th-percentile 1.99 seconds

metric_name: Analytical model accuracy vs measurement — VAX 9000
metric_value: Model predicted 62-70 TPS (high end 70); actual audited result 69.4 TPS; within 1 percent of high-end prediction
notes: Multi-level analytical model validated on VAX 9000 Model 210 TPC-A; model used before hardware available using estimated parameters from VAX 6000 observations

metric_name: Analytical model accuracy vs measurement — VAX 4000 Model 300
metric_value: Model predicted 20.8 TPS; actual audited result 21.5 TPS; within 7 percent of model prediction
notes: Table 8: model prediction 20.8 vs measured 21.5 MQTh; validates assumption on direct memory interconnect vs XMI bus in VAX 6000 Model 400

metric_value: >90 percent
notes: Both VAX 9000 and VAX 4000 Model 300 achieved well over 90 percent CPU utilization at maximum qualified throughput; no other bottleneck

metric_name: KODA group commit: Commit-Lock Design throughput ceiling
metric_value: 300 TPS
notes: VAX 6340 4-processor system (14.4 VUPs total); transaction processing bottlenecked at 300 TPS with basic Commit-Lock Design; upper group size limit of 50

metric_name: KODA group commit: Commit-Stall Design maximum throughput
metric_value: 464 TPS
notes: 65 servers, 30 ms commit stall, 350% CPU utilization; significant improvement over Commit-Lock Design; optimal parameters: 65 servers, 30 ms stall

metric_name: KODA group commit: Willing-to-Wait Design maximum throughput
metric_value: 500 TPS
notes: 50 servers, 90 ms WTW stall, 340% CPU utilization; 66 percent improvement over Commit-Lock Design; maximum group size observed was 45

metric_value: Up to 66 percent improvement in transaction throughput using more efficient grouping designs
notes: Preliminary results comparing Willing-to-Wait (500 TPS) vs Commit-Lock (300 TPS) on VAX 6340; authors optimistic further investigation yields optimal commit sequence

metric_value: System comprises two duplicate zones (Zone A and Zone B); each zone is fully functional computer; connected by duplicate cross-link cables; dual-rail + single-rail design
notes: Four processors total (two per zone) appear to OS as single logical CPU; hardware supplies detection and recovery for CPU/memory faults; FTSS software handles I/O recovery

metric_value: Designed to recover from any single point of hardware failure; fault tolerance provided transparently for all VMS applications
notes: Hardware fault-insertion points built directly into system custom chips; both transient and solid faults testable; VLSI feature size made external fault insertion impractical

metric_value: Phase 1: hardware simulation with fault insertion; Phase 2: hardware verification with system exerciser and fault insertion; Phase 3: system software verification with fault insertion; Phase 4: application verification with fault insertion
notes: More than 500 directed error test cases developed for gate-level system simulation; Phase 4 used DebitCredit benchmark on DECintact to validate application-level transparency

metric_value: Uses snapshot transactions for online backup; parallel transaction recovery; on-line backup, verification and repair utilities; AIJ (after-image journal) files for roll-forward
notes: Raghavan and Rengarajan paper; database availability defined as ability to execute transactions; layers of availability: application, DBMS, OS, hardware

metric_value: Rdb/VMS and VAX DBMS share KODA kernel providing transaction capabilities and commit processing; data access independent of data model
notes: KODA handles all I/O, buffer management, concurrency control, transaction consistency, locking, journaling, and access methods; provides code sharing between relational and network database products

metric_value: Communication managers separated from transaction managers; communication manager propagates 2PC messages to remote nodes; enables multiple commit protocols (IBM SNA LU6.2, OSI-TP) simultaneously
notes: Distinction between communication manager and transaction manager is key architectural decision enabling multi-vendor interoperability

metric_name: VAXcluster node failover for database availability
metric_value: When a node fails, another node detects failure and rolls back lost transactions from failed node; database available as soon as recovery complete
notes: Raghavan and Rengarajan paper; DBR (Database Recovery) process started by monitor; only restriction is brief freeze during which other processes cannot acquire locks held by failed transactions

metric_name: DECintact electronic funds transfer use case
metric_value: DECintact used in banking: data entry clerks, verify/repair application, Fedwire Xmt queue sending to Federal Reserve; demonstrates exactly-once semantics for distributed queues
notes: Store-and-forward processing, deferred processing, and interprocess communication as DECintact queue capabilities; remote queues behave as local queues from application perspective

metric_value: ACMS concentrates security on back-end using VMS ACLs on tasks; SYSUAF-based login; execution controller with access-control-list checking per task; dynamic application changes without stopping work
notes: Contrasted with DECintact which concentrates security at front-end sign-in and menu generation; ACMS model assumed same agency manages VMS and TP security

metric_value: User security profiles, terminal security profiles, and GEOG attribute provide geographic entitlement: certain functions accessible only from specific terminals (e.g., bank officer sensitive functions only at home office)
notes: Frequently a requirement in financial environments; DECintact security independent of VMS SYSUAF; password revalidation attribute per menu function

metric_value: TPC-A represents limited class of applications; additional benchmarks representing broader range of commercial applications expected to be standardized by TPC in coming years
notes: Kohler et al. conclusion; single transaction type provides simple repeatable unit of work but does not represent entire range of TP environments

metric_value: 90 percent of transactions must have response time less than 2 seconds; end-to-end response time (user at terminal); maximum qualified throughput (MQTh) is key metric
notes: TPC Benchmark A specification defines minimum constraint; Digital's measurement results: VAX 9000 avg 1.20 sec, 90th pct 1.74 sec, max 5.82 sec

metric_name: DECtp open-standards alignment will enable market leadership
metric_value: This combination of architecture, software, hardware technology, and support for emerging industry standards places Digital in excellent position to become industry leader for distributed portable transaction processing systems
notes: Borgialli Foreword; prediction of market leadership via standards-based open architecture; DEC ultimately acquired by Compaq 1998 before achieving this goal

metric_value: DEC acquired by Compaq in 1998; did not achieve stated goal of TP market leadership; Oracle, IBM, and Tandem/HP dominated TP markets through 1990s; actual-outcome for OBS-033
notes: DEC acquired by Compaq in 1998; did not achieve stated goal of TP market leadership; Oracle, IBM, and Tandem/HP dominated TP markets through 1990s; actual-outcome for OBS-033

metric_value: Key requirements for 100-year mean time between failures: software-fault containment using processes and software-fault masking using process checkpointing and transactions
notes: Laing Johnson Landau paper citing recent discussions on reliability targets; motivated embedding transactions in VMS kernel

metric_name: Commit processing fraction of TP transaction cost
metric_value: For short TP transactions (modify 1 record): commit processing represents 36 percent of total transaction duration; for batch transactions (modify 500 records): commit processing only 0.2 percent
notes: Spiro Joshi Rengarajan paper; four transaction phases: start, data manipulation, logging, commit; illustrates why commit optimization critical for OLTP workloads

metric_name: DECtp convergence of ACMS and DECintact
metric_value: Set of common features shared by both monitors growing with latest releases; external convergence fostered by internal convergence sharing underlying code; investment in either monitor protected
notes: Speer and Storm conclusion; distinguishing feature will be different application programming interfaces (4GL vs 3GL); functional differences are ones of emphasis not incompatibility

metric_value: Banking, laboratory automation, manufacturing, government, insurance, retail, health care, transportation, communications
notes: Borgialli Foreword; TP described as information lifeline supporting achievement of daily business objectives and competitive advantage

metric_value: Many older TP systems centralized and tied to particular vendor; as expansion continues beyond geographic boundaries, centralized single-vendor TP systems less able to offer needed flexibility
notes: Borgialli Foreword; positions DECtp distributed open-standards approach as solution to centralized proprietary mainframe TP (implicitly IBM)

metric_value: $START_TRANSW, $END_TRANSW, $ADD_BRANCHW, $START_BRANCHW, $READY_TO_COMMITW, $DECLARE_RMW, $JOIN_RMW, $FINISH_RMOPW system service calls
notes: Pseudocode illustrating use of DECdtm services for distributed transaction spanning two applications on different nodes; IPC mechanism for cross-node communication
